summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the romanticized notion of marathon coding sessions as a badge of honor. He cites Steve McConnell's Code Complete, which dismantles Edward Yourdon's praise of 16-hour coding days and sleepless nights as 'programming machismo.' McConnell argues that these heroic efforts typically create more defects than they solve, requiring weeks of cleanup afterward. Atwood admits he sometimes falls into this trap himself but emphasizes that enthusiasm, while valuable, must not substitute for competent, sustainable work practices.
key_points: All-night coding sessions feel heroic but often introduce defects that take weeks to fix
Enthusiasm for programming is valuable but becomes counterproductive when it replaces competence
Time invested does not automatically equal value produced
The 'macho coder' mentality is seductive but ultimately harmful
Working with passionate people beats apathy, but passion needs boundaries
money_quotes: Those all-night programming stints make you feel like the greatest programmer in the world, but then you have to spend several weeks correcting the defects you installed during your blaze of glory.
This lusty tribute to programming machismo is pure B.S. and an almost certain recipe for failure.
Excitement is no substitute for competency. Remember which is more important.
I'd rather work with someone who is gonzo than someone who is apathetic – but there's definitely a point where it becomes a negative.
Don't invest tons of hours for the wrong reasons.
Time does not always equal value.
key_insight: Heroic all-night coding sessions are counterproductive theater that creates more problems than they solve, making sustainable competence far more valuable than dramatic enthusiasm.
